FULL CONTENT:
By[Anthony Wing Kosner](https://www.forbes.com/sites/anthonykosner/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/anthonykosner/")

# IndieBio Accelerator Helps Synthetic Biology Attain The Agility Of Software Startups

By[Anthony Wing Kosner](https://www.forbes.com/sites/anthonykosner/ "https://www.forbes.com/sites/anthonykosner/"),

Former Contributor.

[IndieBio](http://sf.indie.bio/ "indie bio, san francisco"), the San Francisco accelerator for biology-based startups [announced yesterday](http://sf.indie.bio/faq/ "indiebio faq about funding") that it will be increasing the funding for each company to $250,000. This is more than twice the amount that rival accelerator [Y Combinator](https://www.ycombinator.com/ "y combinator accelerator") makes available to seed its startups. IndieBio plans to hatch 30 companies per year in its San Francisco location. (It has a second location in Cork, Ireland which is not part of this funding arrangement.)

The next wave of biotech is taking off, but traditionally these companies have required a lot of time and money to get to a viable product. Along with that quarter of a million dollar seed funding, each IndieBio batch runs for 130 days, which is longer than the typical 3-month accelerator program. "This is an experiment to understand how long of a runway is just right," says IndiBio founder Arvind Gupta. "We are learning and will evolve the model to find the optimal balance of funding and risk tolerance for radical ideas."

A key to the success of the program is the state-of-the-art lab in which the teams grow their products. I visited the lab recently and found it bustling and cozy. IndieBio allocates just six feet of lab space per participant and the density is highly intentional. They modeled the lab on neighboring [UCSF](https://www.ucsf.edu/about/translational-medicine/research "ucsf research"), which is "one of the largest and most productive [research centers] in the country." But program director Ryan Bethencourt points out that, “We are seeing that a well-stocked physical lab for startups isn’t enough to take them from prototype to commercial viability. It takes more money and time, particularly in the Bay Area to create a product.”

![indiebio-logo-labs](https://blogs-images.forbes.com/anthonykosner/files/2015/05/indiebio-logo-labs.jpg)

![indiebio-logo-labs](https://blogs-images.forbes.com/anthonykosner/files/2015/05/indiebio-logo-labs.jpg)

The combination of a longer runway, a turnkey lab and world-class mentoring should prove catalyzing for these startups. But this is just the beginning. It will take a handful of high-visibility startup companies that can demonstrate how the new synthetic biology can improve people's lives to confront popular misconceptions about the dangers and scariness of biotech. It's not all Monsanto and DuPont; it's also a lot of well-meaning biohackers who genuinely want to spread the power of biology as widely and transparently as possible.

The founders I met at IndieBio have that dream. They are using all of the tools of contemporary technology—big data analytics, DNA sequencing, machine vision, 3D printing, robotics and more—to make products that will have a positive impact on health and the environment. Some projects involve lowering the cost and increasing the quality of materials that can heal our bodies, like stem cells and wound dressings. Others involve increasing the accuracy (and lowering the costs) of diagnosing illness and delivering targeted treatments. One notable project is growing egg whites in the lab to meet the growing demand without having to raise more chickens. Still others are making tools that democratize the means of biological production itself.

[Are they tinkering with nature? Yes. Should we be scared? Not really.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.forbes.com%2Fsites%2Fanthonykosner%2F2015%2F05%2F13%2Findiebio-accelerator-helps-synthetic-biology-attain-the-agility-of-software-startups%2F&text=Are%20%23biohackers%20tinkering%20with%20nature%3F%20Yes.%20Should%20we%20be%20scared%3F%20Not%20really.%20%40indbio)  As [Bethencourt wrote recently](http://www.forbes.com/sites/techonomy/2015/02/19/ubiquitous-biotech-in-a-time-of-ignorance/ "ryan bethencourt in technonomy"), "Human cells are outnumbered some three to 10 times by bacterial cells in and on our bodies. And it's estimated that at least 11 percent of the human genome is viral in origin. We are the original genetically modified organisms."

Gupta claims that, “ [Biology is now accelerating at a pace faster than Moore’s Law](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.forbes.com%2Fsites%2Fanthonykosner%2F2015%2F05%2F13%2Findiebio-accelerator-helps-synthetic-biology-attain-the-agility-of-software-startups%2F&text=Biology%20is%20now%20accelerating%20at%20a%20pace%20faster%20than%20Moore%E2%80%99s%20Law%2C%20say%20%40arvndgpta) ." He sees accelerators like IndiBio as an entrepreneurial alternative for the young biologists that are creating an "engine to drive Silicon Valley into the next century.” Chief Science Officer Ron Shigeta thinks all of this care and tending will enable these biohackers to “focus on building world-changing companies.” The agility that has allowed software startups to create so much value is now available to biologists to invent a new kind of biotech. "We are doing this to help founders get to market quicker and help them retain control of their companies—more like tech and less like old biotech," Gupta tells me. Look for the biology major to follow the upward arc of computer science in the coming years.

# Innovating with agility, deep tech, and AI: New from MIT Sloan Management Review

By

Brian Eastwood

Oct 7, 2024

## Why It Matters

![](/themes/custom/mitsloan/img/anchorlink-icon.png)

Large enterprises don’t have to move slowly. Here are three ways to unlock fresh innovation.

Innovation can take many forms in the enterprise. The latest insights from MIT Sloan Management Review offer leaders three approaches to increasing innovation: enabling organizational agility, pursuing deep-tech ventures, and committing to best-in-class artificial intelligence products.

## **4 guardrails to empower agility in the enterprise**

Many leaders would like their large firm to be as nimble as a startup. But many also face the challenges of risk aversion, shared decision-making, and the need to align innovation efforts with strategic objectives or shareholder expectations.

Nick van der Meulen, a research scientist at the [MIT Center for Information Systems Research](https://cisr.mit.edu/), explains how enterprises can encourage organizational agility, proactivity, and adaptability by putting in place [four guardrails that define how employees should pursue innovation](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/the-four-guardrails-that-enable-agility/).

[Read: The four guardrails that enable agility](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/the-four-guardrails-that-enable-agility/)

## **Deep tech can catalyze long-term innovation**

Many of the problems today’s enterprises face — including sustainability, labor shortages, and ongoing market shifts — can’t be solved with a simple digital offering. These challenges need an infusion of deep tech, broadly defined as “a category of solutions rooted in atoms rather than bits,” such as quantum computing, synthetic biology, or other innovations that emerge from the research lab.

MIT Sloan associate dean Fiona Murray writes that [deep-tech startups hold promise for industries that tread lightly in R&D](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/why-you-should-tap-innovation-at-deep-tech-startups/), such as financial services, retail, and infrastructure. But these sectors, along with health care delivery and government services, aren’t always accustomed to working with emerging technologies. Along with co-authors [Martin Murmann](https://www.bfh.ch/en/about-bfh/people/fkhsbmpehynm/) and [Stefan Raff](https://www.bfh.ch/en/about-bfh/people/45ygpugvl34q/) (both of the Bern University of Applied Sciences), Murray identifies three challenges for deep-tech ventures, along with best practices for managing them.

[Read: Why you should tap innovation at deep tech startups](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/why-you-should-tap-innovation-at-deep-tech-startups/)

## **Innovative, responsible AI tools at Scotiabank**

Scotiabank, like many financial institutions, has embraced generative AI and built a chatbot for its contact center. But few others have received the same level of recognition as the Canadian bank, which has won awards for innovation and AI ethics. [MIT Initiative on the Digital Economy](https://ide.mit.edu/) fellow [Thomas H. Davenport](https://ide.mit.edu/people/thomas-davenport/) and co-author [Randy Bean](https://www.linkedin.com/in/randybeannvp/) explain [how Scotiabank built its chatbot](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/how-scotiabank-built-an-ethical-engaged-ai-culture/) and made it stand out.

The bank took a collaborative approach to developing the chatbot, with designers working alongside engineers and data scientists. The model was trained using an “AI-for-AI” strategy, with automated workflows for curating its knowledge base and identifying training topics. Developers did their best to curate unstructured data before feeding it into the model. Scotiabank also worked with Deloitte Canada to develop an application that assesses the ethical impact of an AI use case early on; the bank has since required anyone doing advanced analytics work to participate in data ethics education.

While Accenture and DataIQ have recognized the bank’s efforts, the chatbot’s results speak for themselves. Since the chatbot’s debut in late 2022, its accuracy level has increased from 35% to 90%. More than 40% of customers get their questions answered without human intervention. And when human agents need to step in, the chatbot’s automatically generated summary of the online conversation reduces resolution times as much as 70%.

[Read: How Scotiabank built an ethical, engaged AI culture](https://sloanreview.mit.edu/article/how-scotiabank-built-an-ethical-engaged-ai-culture/)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Synthetic biology start-ups leverage agility similar to tech firms, allowing rapid adaptation to market changes and innovative shifts, fostering an environment conducive to breakthrough discoveries.
Start-ups utilize advanced tools for swift prototyping and testing, significantly reducing time from concept to viable product, enhancing their ability to innovate and iterate quickly.
Start-ups often collaborate with academic and research institutions, gaining access to cutting-edge technology, expertise, and resources that accelerate product development and scientific validation.

Innovative funding models and accelerators, like IndieBio, provide vital financial support and resources, enabling start-ups to scale operations and focus on ambitious projects without traditional constraints.

Synthetic biology start-ups target specific niche markets, addressing unique needs and challenges, which allows them to capture specialized audiences and differentiate themselves in the competitive landscape.
